speaker: interviewer
text: I have an interview with Alberta Szalita. Today is December 11, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, so there are two things that I wanted to indicate from that period. First of all, my mother was away for some six months. So we were left, left alone. The maid who came from Luck to Rostov to work for us got involved with a-- with a character who was-- must have been a revolutionary, a participator.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somebody was telling them about-- somebody must have told them about us. And what that-- at that, that particular time, that was not yet when my father was sick. So what they did is we had a lot of winter clothing first, underneath.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put it in the bed in case they will come to, to the house to, to rob us, so to, to protect those things. And they put me in bed to, to say that I'm sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were two beds-- the bedroom for the parents. In one, they put the women's fur coats. And the other one, under the mattress, they put the male-- and I was lying in bed as if I am sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came in, those-- a dozen of them, maybe. And they said, the, the girl is sick. So he said, I already had typhus-- that-- a man, one of the soldiers. So he took me in his hands and put me in this other bed. And he took from under that whatever was in that bed-- took it away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they called my-- they told my father that he should follow them. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and my brother went after them to ask them to leave my-- to-- not to shoot my father. I, in meantime, stayed in this other bed, half-undressed. And I remember that I was praying Shema Yisrael. And I was praying loud. And I was terribly upset about the whole situation. And my brother afterward was teasing me, [NON-ENGLISH]. He made out of that-- as if I was doing something wrong. In other words, he, he laughed at me because I was praying. Well, I don't know whether I ever prayed like this a second time in my life. I-- but it, it became like-- as if it was a kind of joke for me to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I might have been nine or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --something like this, I think, if it was in 1919 or something like that. But I forgot when I was talking about the shtetl, about my grandmother. You reminded me that my, my father's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's mother, in this new house that they built after the fire, she had a separate-- a separate wing. She had a separate kitchen, a dining room, a room. And everybody was terribly scared of her-- me, too. And my father was very much-- very careful about, well, his mother. He loved her. He, he protected her. He was, like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] treated her with enormous respect. She came-- she came-- my, my, my grandfather was a widow-- was widowed. He had two daughters from his first marriage. And they had children. And they got married. And when they got married-- the two daughters-- that's when he remarried. And he remarried that woman that came from-- she was from the family of Chaim [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feygel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Feygel.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where my younger sister-- when she was born and my father gave her the name Fanny, that was, was coming from this Feygel, Feygel. And my grandmother died. I remember her death, that she died a-- the year that we left, left the town. And the reason I'm saying it is that her-- her existence and her death had a very enormous impression on me that I'll talk about it when I get to-- to becoming a psychiatrist. I'm just filling in for that what I missed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, so they took away the-- some of the men's clothing which was in the bed that I was lying. Those-- they didn't shot-- they didn't shoot my father. And he, afterwards, had to be-- afterwards in that house, they had to take turns in, in, in standing watch, not to let somebody come in into the courtyard. So he had to put a, a woman's fur coat because the men warm clothing was, was taken away by that-- by those robbers. Well, in Rostov, sometimes during the, the fights that were going on-- and-- and I remember this-- the red sky at night because there was so much shooting going on. We used to run away from the apartment and go. There was a bakery in the courtyard, in our building there where we lived-- not in the building-- buildings there were around.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were hiding there in the bakery from the shooting. So we had these fights going on around Rostov. And I remember them. I visualize them. But I'm not going to go in and detail them. It all calmed down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- I remember my mother coming back from the trip that she made. And she left with dark hair. She came back with white hair. She had hair like the color like mine now. But when she came, life changed in terms of being better taken care of and all that. And life went on. I went in Rostov to three gymnasiums because my mother-- it was my mother's-- my mother's initiative that I should start studying in summertime when there is a vacation and skip a class-- and skip a class. So the result was that at the time when we went back to-- home, I was advanced in gymnasium there. I was finishing sixth class. I would have probably graduated-- in a year or two, I would have graduated from high school. But when we arrived to-- back home, that shtetl Wlodzimierzec became Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That means that we had to start-- study Polish language in order to, to get to school and to catch up with the difference in the education-- Polish education and the Russian education. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The, the way we went back home is that my, my mother's brother sent papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, where was he sending--

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the papers from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Luck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we packed and went-- didn't have much problems getting back to-- back home. We went by train to Minsk. I remember in Minsk, we spent a, a, a month waiting for get-- to get together papers, exactly. That's what we were sitting there. And Minsk is Belarussia. Belarus, it's now. Now, it's a separate-- but it's over, the czars. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] We came-- we came back-- we came back in the fall of, of '22. And we were in the-- our old house in Wlodzimierzec, the same Wlodzimierzec. And we spent a year there, had the teacher that taught-- we-- three of us girls. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember whether Mina also was in that group. We had the one teacher, all of us, to learn Polish. She knew Polish German. But this teacher, [NON-ENGLISH], was teaching us Polish. And it was clear that we are not going to be able to, to continue education if we stay in our little shtetl. So we-- my, my mother went, went to-- a couple of times to Luck where her parents lived. And then they started to look where we should settle, and-- and what they should do. And in the meantime, I got reacquainted with this, this little town of Wlodzimierzec, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the group of girls and, and boys who were our own age. So again, life went on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we settled in a town, which was called Volodymyr-Volynskyi. Now Volodymyr, it's like your old 'Volodymyr-itz'-- Wlodzimierzec. Now, you see, I'm mixing already the names from Russian to Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Volodymyr-Volynskyi, that's where we went to gymnasium. That's where we got settled. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was now Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was now Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we are back. We left Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left the, the, the Reds. We, we had the Whites because the Whites were a long time in Rostov. Relatively speaking, it took longer. [NON-ENGLISH]-- I remember the names of the generals. I remember the-- I was reading papers. And I remember that Wilson and Lloyd George and Poincare what-- they were all that-- because they [INAUDIBLE] have discussions in the house about it. And in Wlodzimierz, we spent another year. First it was a year in, in Wlodzimierzec, and then a year in Wlodzimierz in order to-- to catch up, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] catch up with, with both, with their schooling and the language. I have to tell you that the-- that the time in Rostov in gymnasium, [INAUDIBLE] we did not have too much of studying going on, serious study.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a lot of time was also spent to teach us these revolutionary songs and to, to march on the-- in May, May 1st marches, and all that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it, it was a-- we, we needed really a lot of, of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a lot of work to put into really to be able to-- to get to, to start schooling in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I from-- that I was [INAUDIBLE] supposedly finishing the sixth class in, in Rostov, got into the fifth class of gymnasium. And in that gymnasium, we, we had an audition to other things. There was Latin that one needed to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my parents, in Volodymyr-Volynskyi, they did open a, a store of, of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what-- things that you buy to make some frock-- well, dresses or, or, or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] materials.

speaker: interviewer
text: Textile.

speaker: interviewee
text: Textile--

speaker: interviewer
text: Textile.

speaker: interviewee
text: --exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they opened a, a store of textiles. But it was not successful. I mean, they didn't know how to make a-- to, to, to make a living, really, neither my father nor my mother. The house was sold, the one that we had in, in, in this Wlodzimierzec. And then gradually, this-- the money was spent. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So pretty soon, my mother had to leave-- go back to Luck. And she moved in with her parents. And my father got a job in, in Brest-Litovsk, thanks to that cousin that was a cousin of the family that was also in Rostov. And they came back to, to Brest, which was called Brzesc at that time because it was Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to give lessons to support myself and remained in this Wlodzimierz. And I finished at their gymnasium in 1929. And I was already supporting myself a, a couple of years. And I also took my sister from Luck to come to, to this Wlodzimierz. And so they finished the school there. And that again, there's a lot to be told about that particular period. But in 1929, I got my, my matura-- you know what that is, matura--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and went to Warsaw. To-- in, in Warsaw, lived my father's sister, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I should tell you that my father was born an uncle. You know, there was my grandfather-- my father's father got married when his two older daughters were married already and would-- had children. Then he married this aunt-- this grandmother of mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when he was born, he had already nephew-- a nephew, nine years old, and a niece, seven years old. That's the one that also lived in Rostov-on-the-Don and then returned at approximately the same time as we returned home from Rostov. They returned also to-- to Brest, where they were living. So he worked there in Brest. And he would travel, but come home from time to time. And he had the job there. And the job was a bookkeeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I, I went to Warsaw in 1929, and applied to medical school. That aunt of mine who lived-- his sister-- lived in Warsaw. When they heard that I wanted to go to medical school, they, they almost laughed into my face. They didn't believe that it was possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in the meantime, you have to know that the Jews were not so easily accepted in Poland to-- here we go back to the business of the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I, I applied to this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, to the medical faculty of the Warsaw University. And to my surprise-- I don't know how much I was surprised, but everybody else was surprised-- I was accepted to study medicine. At that time, my father was working in Brest. He, he was-- undertook that he will send me every month, 150 zlotys. That was zlotys, Polish money. But after the first month that he sent me this money, he wrote a letter that he's not going to be able to-- to send me money. That was 1929. That was the year of the Depression all over. It was a depression in this country, and it was Depression that was there even from before.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I was, left to my own devices in, in, in Warsaw. And, well, anyway, I studied six, six years medical. I did the medical studies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I supported myself. In order to support myself, I-- what I was able to do is, I was giving in-- a notice in, in the Warsaw papers. There was a, a Jewish paper in Polish, [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was which was a Polish paper. A student of medicine teaches, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] teaches Latin, mathematics, and physics. And I used to get pupils. And then-- and then, well, that's what I did for a while. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, yeah, what I wanted to say-- so I met one from, from Wlodzimierz, a, a woman. [? Kosovska ?] was her name. She studied-- whom I knew. She studied to be a teacher-- met her in the streets. And she had worked-- she, she had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a-- some kind of a relative of hers that had this store on Krucha, Krucha in Warsaw-- Krucha, the street, K-R-U-C-H-A. Had the store of women's clothing and underwear and all that. And she used this as a, a living quarter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because they wanted to have someone sleep in the store so that nobody should steal in the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she, she was finished-- she finished seminary to be a teacher. And she said, how would you like to take over my-- this position-- that position to be a night watcher at the store? So she-- I said, well, I, I will be glad to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, so she introduced me to those owners. And they liked this idea. They liked me. So now-- so I got that-- a big [POINTING] key, like that size, to, to be able to enter that store or come and go-- and could use it to sleep there. Because during the daytime they had the, the-- their business going. And I liked it there. It was a living quarter. I gave it the name El Dorado.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And, as I say, I had this large key. But in daytime, if I had some time of-- free time. So I used to go to the library on [NON-ENGLISH] Street. Well, I like very much that Warsaw. And I got to know very well the city. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, so I went on studying medicine. So the main thing, of course, in the-- during these years was to, to make a living. Studying was like secondary. It was not that the first thing was to study. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew-- I did not mention that at all, but I knew languages. And in addition to studying Hebrew, as I did very solidly in the beginning of my life till, till the age of 11, in, in Rostov-on-the-Don, we had in the gymnasium, German and French. Depending which school, and one-- and sometimes, it was both of these languages. And in Volodymyr-Volynskyi, we had English as a foreign, foreign language. And plus I was interested in languages. So I used to read books from-- with the-- with a dictionary. And so, so that was the beginning. And while I was in Warsaw, I continued to study English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the third year of medicine-- or maybe that was the end of the third year of medicine, one of my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] partners in the medical school, she knew German. And I knew Russian. And I knew Russian and Polish and French and German and English. So we started to translate-- summarize medical literature from foreign languages into Polish. So instead of lessons, as a way of making a living, we did-- and I-- she did translate from the German language because that's the language she knew, this partner of mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: In medical school, you do certain things, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she was my partner. And she was from-- from Lodz. And I got to know her friends from Lodz. So we became very friendly with each other-- so in this way, helping each other, so to speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I, I was translating from American literature, the German Medical and American Medical Association from from London, from from Paris, and Russian literature into, into Polish. So it done-- it done two things for me. First of all, I knew-- I learned more about what goes on in medicine. And secondly, it gave me income that I needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, my sisters gradually got their matura. And they came to Warsaw. I brought them over to Warsaw. So this El Dorado was going from, from one for-- to the next. And we used to-- we used to even make little receptions there in the store at night in the evening when the store was closed. Subsequently, again when I was in the third year, I, I got one job where I was helping a-- an only girl. She was Schwarzman. She lived with her father on Kredytowa, a very elegant street in-- in Warsaw. I got the-- what they called in Polish, [POLISH]-- it means a condition-- that I was helping her with her studies. And I had a room for myself and had a place to live. So that's exactly when my, my sisters could use that store. And I didn't need to use that store anymore. And subsequently, I was able to rent a room, first with one other student, and then-- and subsequently, for myself a room in-- in apartments. People were, were subletting their room to students in order to get some income from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just tell us-- being Jewish student in the medical school, there was no problem for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was no problem for me. But you felt it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, you, you started to feel it when you entered the medical school-- to Jewish students. In 1929, you start-- in medical school, you start from anatomy. So anatomy, you have to cut a cadaver. And the, the Jews-- the Jews were not given their cadavers so easily for such things because there was the-- there was always this Chevra Kadisha, or whatever they are called, that were picking up somebody who died and, and, and buried them. This was their explanation. So, in other words, they gave us little babies to cut. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I know the first day, I didn't go to, to this prosectorium. I was afraid. At that time, the whole idea of cutting a cadaver was not such a, a thing that one was looking forward to do. And then I went to the prosectorium. And [POINTING] I got a little baby like that size. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, of course, it, it upset me. It upset me so that when I make tea in the evening in this El Dorado, and the, the kettle was singing, it, it was like, for me, as if the child is crying-- I mean, in my imagination. But again, you had-- you had to do it. You had to do it. But this-- the, the Polish students had the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] big cadavers. And we, the, the Jews, had to have-- that was the only thing that I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many, would you think-- how many students that you knew were Jewish at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the examination-- the examination for this-- the application to medical school, I just didn't say anything about it. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were for 20 seats or 20-something that they accepted Jews, there were over 400 people that applied to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to that examination.
